Tom Hardy, The Raid? Say Less

Good news, Gareth Evans is interested in working on his third film 'The Raid'!
After deciding to leave the 'The Raid 3' project for dozens of years because he was no longer interested because the plot he was working on seemed forced and prolonged the conclusion, it turns out that now Gareth Evans is interested in continuing his third film.
This was revealed by the director during an interview with Entertainment Weekly to promote his latest action film for Netflix, namely 'Havoc'.
Gareth also said that at that time he felt that the plot he had would ruin The Raid, especially when he started working on other projects that kept him busy and his interests shifted to new projects with much fresher stories.
However, still during the interview session, Gareth Evans finally gave a surprise that he is currently developing a new plot and concept for the film 'The Raid 3'.
Gareth said that all this happened because recently, he returned to Jakarta to reunite with his old friends when he lived in Jakarta, especially he also met again with his old partner, Iko Uwais.
Gareth also admitted to being surprised, after 7 years of not meeting Iko Uwais face to face, he saw such rapid development of Iko Uwais who now has more work experience in the film industry, is increasingly varied and more capable.
Because of a long chat with Iko during the reunion, he finally thought of a new plot and concept for The Raid 3. After returning from Jakarta, he developed the new plot and concept of The Raid 3 with Iko Uwais' current development.
However, Gareth also said that he is still developing the plot and concept slowly. If it turns out that when it is finished, it turns out that the new plot and concept still seem forced like the plot he wrote 10 years ago, then he decided not to make it.

**⭐ 1 Star – "Havoc: A Movie That Lives Up to Its Name (But Not in a Good Way)"**

Save your $3.99. *Havoc* isn't a gritty action film — it's a sloppy parade of endless bullets, laughable fight scenes, and grainy visuals that feel like they were filmed through a dirty windshield.

Tom Hardy does his best to carry this mess, but even he can’t save it. Watching a 120-pound character toss Hardy around like a ragdoll was pure cartoon nonsense. Add in pistols firing like belt-fed machine guns and Uzis that apparently hold thousands of rounds without ever reloading, and you get a movie so detached from reality it becomes a comedy — unintentionally.

The plot is generic. The action is sped-up and shaky to cover the lack of real choreography. And the "gritty" filter slapped over every scene just makes it painful to watch.

I didn’t rent a movie — I paid for a 90-minute endurance test. Skip it.
